steps.title: Tradities
steps.desc: Dutch traditions are unique. Sinterklaas, Koningsdag, Carnaval, the Nieuwjaarsduik — they define Dutch culture. Let's learn the vocabulary to talk about what makes the Netherlands special.

steps.note:
THE Dutch children's holiday.
'Pakjesavond' = gift evening (Dec 5th, not 25th!).
'Pepernoten' = spiced cookies.
Much bigger than Christmas in NL for children.

steps.exampleEn: On King's Day everyone wears orange.
steps.note:
April 27th. The king's birthday.
'Vrijmarkt' = free market (everyone sells stuff).
The whole country turns orange.
'Koninginnedag' was the old name (Queen's Day).

steps.example: Carnaval wordt vooral in het zuiden gevierd.
steps.exampleEn: Carnival is mainly celebrated in the south.
steps.note:
3 days before Ash Wednesday.
Mainly in the south: Brabant, Limburg.
Costumes, parades, beer. Cities get silly nicknames.
'Alaaf!' = carnival greeting in Limburg.

May 5th. End of WWII occupation.
Day before: 'Dodenherdenking' (Remembrance Day, May 4).
At 8pm on May 4: 2 minutes of silence nationwide.

steps.exampleEn: Thousands of people do the New Year's dive.
steps.note:
January 1st: jump into the North Sea.
Scheveningen beach: the biggest one.
Water temperature: about 5°C!
'Duiken' = to dive.

steps.example: Bij Oud en Nieuw eten we oliebollen.
steps.exampleEn: At New Year's we eat oliebollen.
steps.note:
Deep-fried dough balls with powdered sugar.
'Oud en Nieuw' = Old and New (New Year's Eve).
Also: 'appelflappen' = apple turnovers.
Traditional New Year's food.

steps.exampleEn: How do you celebrate your birthday?
steps.note:
'Vieren' = to celebrate.
'Het feest' = the party/celebration.
'Gefeliciteerd!' = Congratulations!
Dutch birthdays: YOU bring cake to work.

steps.s: Bij Oud en Nieuw eten we ___.
steps.hint: Deep-fried dough balls for New Year's

steps.q: On Dutch birthdays, who brings the cake?
steps.hint: YOU bring cake — to work, to school, everywhere

steps.title: Het Milieu
steps.desc: The Netherlands takes sustainability seriously. Solar panels, cycling infrastructure, recycling — but also a country below sea level dealing with climate change. The vocabulary of environment and sustainability.

steps.note:
Het-word. False friend alert!
'Milieu' = environment (not 'milieu' as in social class).
'Het milieu beschermen' = to protect the environment.

'Duurzaam' = sustainable/durable.
'Duurzaamheid' = sustainability.
'Duurzame energie' = sustainable energy.
A key Dutch buzzword.

steps.exampleEn: In the Netherlands we recycle a lot.
steps.note:
Borrowed from English. Also: 'hergebruiken' (re-use).
'De afvalbak' = the waste bin.
'Gescheiden inzameling' = separated collection.

De-word. 'Vervuilen' = to pollute.
'De luchtvervuiling' = air pollution.
'De watervervuiling' = water pollution.

'Besparen' = to save (reduce usage).
NOT 'sparen' which = to save (put money aside).
'Energiebesparing' = energy saving.

steps.example: Steeds meer mensen fietsen naar het werk.
steps.exampleEn: More and more people cycle to work.
steps.note:
Very useful connector!
'Steeds' = ever/continually.
'Steeds meer' = more and more.
'Steeds minder' = less and less.

steps.s: ___ meer mensen fietsen naar het werk.

opts: besparen = reduce usage, sparen = put money aside
steps.ans: besparen = reduce usage, sparen = put money aside

steps.title: De Werkende Vrouw
steps.desc: A new grammar pattern: using the PRESENT participle as an adjective. 'De werkende vrouw' = the working woman. 'Een groeiend probleem' = a growing problem. The present participle = infinitive + d, then add -e before nouns.
goals: de werkende vrouw, een groeiend probleem

steps.title: 📐 Present Participle as Adjective
steps.text:
Formation:
Infinitive + -d = present participle
werken + d = werkend (working)
groeien + d = groeiend (growing)
lopen + d = lopend (walking/running)
slapen + d = slapend (sleeping)

As adjective (add -e before a noun):
de werkende vrouw = the working woman
een groeiend probleem = a growing problem
het lopende project = the ongoing project
de slapende baby = the sleeping baby

This describes an ONGOING action:
'werkend' = currently working
'groeiend' = currently growing

Do NOT confuse with past participles:
werkend = working (present, ongoing)
gewerkt = worked (past, completed)

steps.note:
werken + d = werkend.
As adjective: 'de werkende bevolking'.
= the working population.
Describes people who ARE working (ongoing).

steps.example: Een groeiend probleem in de stad.
steps.exampleEn: A growing problem in the city.
steps.note:
groeien + d = groeiend.
'Een groeiend probleem' = a growing problem.
'De groeiende economie' = the growing economy.

steps.example: De komende week ga ik op vakantie.
steps.exampleEn: The coming week I'm going on vacation.
steps.note:
komen + d = komend.
'De komende week' = next week.
'Het komende jaar' = the coming year.
Very commonly used for 'next/upcoming'.

steps.example: Het lopende onderzoek is bijna klaar.
steps.exampleEn: The ongoing research is almost finished.
steps.note:
lopen + d = lopend.
'Het lopende project' = the ongoing/current project.
'Lopende zaken' = ongoing matters/current affairs.

steps.hint: Like regular adjectives: groot → grote, werkend → werkende

steps.s: De {1} week ga ik op vakantie. Het {2} project is bijna klaar.
steps.hint: The coming week. The ongoing project.
Before a noun: werkend + e = werkende

steps.title: De Gesloten Deur
steps.desc: Now the PAST participle as an adjective. You already know past participles from the perfect tense: gesloten, geschreven, gebakken. Now use them before nouns: 'de gesloten deur' = the closed door.
goals: de gesloten deur, een gebakken ei
Contrast: present vs past participle adjectives

steps.title: 📐 Past Participle as Adjective
steps.text:
You already know past participles:
gesloten (closed), geschreven (written),
gebakken (baked/fried), gebroken (broken)

Use them as adjectives before nouns:
de gesloten deur = the closed door
het geschreven woord = the written word
een gebakken ei = a fried egg
een gebroken been = a broken leg

Adjective agreement rules apply:
de gesloten deur (de-word → no extra -e!)
een gesloten deur (indefinite → no extra -e!)
het gesloten raam (het-word → no extra -e!)

Wait — no -e? That's because most strong PPs
already end in -en: geslot-EN, geschrev-EN.
They DON'T take an extra -e.

Weak PPs (ending in -t/-d) DO take -e:
de gekookte aardappel = the cooked potato
een georganiseerd feest = an organized party

steps.example: De gesloten deur ging niet open.
steps.note:
sluiten → gesloten.
'De gesloten winkel' = the closed shop.
Strong PP (-en ending): no extra -e needed!

schrijven → geschreven.
'Een geschreven brief' = a written letter.
Strong PP: no extra -e.

bakken → gebakken.
'Een gebakken vis' = a fried fish.
'Gebak' = pastries/cakes (related noun).

breken → gebroken. Cognate!
English: broken, German: gebrochen.
'Een gebroken hart' = a broken heart.

steps.title: 📐 Present vs Past Participle
steps.text:
PRESENT participle = ONGOING action:
de werkende vrouw = the woman who IS working
een groeiend probleem = a problem that IS growing

PAST participle = COMPLETED action/state:
de gesloten deur = the door that HAS BEEN closed
een gebroken been = a leg that HAS BEEN broken

Present: -end(e) = happening NOW.
Past: ge-...-en/-t/-d = already DONE.

de slapende baby = the baby who is sleeping (now)
de gebroken vaas = the vase that was broken (already)

steps.s: De {1} winkel is dicht. Een {2} probleem in de stad.

steps.title: Samengestelde Woorden
steps.desc: Dutch LOVES compound nouns. Stack two, three, or even four words together! 'Zonnebril' (sun-glasses), 'fietsenstalling' (bicycle-parking), 'koffiezetapparaat' (coffee-making-device). Learn the rules and the linking sounds.
goals: Gender rule: last word determines de/het

steps.title: 🔗 Dutch Compound Nouns
steps.text:
Dutch stacks nouns together. No spaces!

zon + bril = zonnebril (sunglasses)
fiets + stalling = fietsenstalling (bicycle parking)
koffie + zet + apparaat = koffiezetapparaat (coffee maker)
huis + arts = huisarts (GP/family doctor)

Linking sounds between the parts:
-e-: zonnebril (zon + e + bril)
-en-: fietsenstalling (fiets + en + stalling)
-s-: dorpsplein (dorp + s + plein)
Nothing: huisarts (huis + arts)

Which linking sound? No perfect rule!
But -en- is common after plural-like nouns.
-s- is common after certain endings.
You'll develop a feel for it.

Gender rule: the LAST word determines de/het!
de zonnebril (bril = de)
het dorpsplein (plein = het)
de huisarts (arts = de)

steps.note:
zon(ne) + bril = sun-glasses.
De-word (because 'de bril' = de).
'Zon' + linking -e- + 'bril'.

steps.example: Ik ga morgen naar de huisarts.
steps.exampleEn: I'm going to the GP tomorrow.
steps.note:
huis + arts = house-doctor.
No linking sound needed.
De-word (because 'de arts' = de).
Everyone in NL has a huisarts.

fiets + en + stalling = bicycle parking.
-en- linking sound.
You'll find these at every train station in NL.

koffie + zet + apparaat = coffee-making-device.
3-part compound! Het-word (apparaat = het).
The most Dutch compound example.
'Zetten' = to make/brew (coffee).

steps.q: In compounds, gender is determined by:
steps.hint: De zonneBRIL (bril = de), het dorpsPLEIN (plein = het)

steps.title: Ver-, Be-, Ont-
steps.desc: Dutch has inseparable prefixes that completely change a verb's meaning. Unlike 'op-' or 'af-' (separable), these prefixes STAY attached. 'Ver-' (change), 'be-' (make transitive), 'ont-' (undo), 'her-' (redo).
goals: Inseparable prefixes: ver-, be-, ont-, her-
Past participle: NO ge-! (betaald, not gebetaald)

steps.title: 📐 Inseparable Prefixes
steps.text:
Separable (you know these): op, af, aan, uit, mee...
'Ik bel je op.' (splits in main clause)

Inseparable (NEW): ver-, be-, ont-, her-
'Ik verander het.' (NEVER splits!)

Key rule for past participles:
Separable: ge- goes between: opgebeld, aangemaakt
Inseparable: NO ge- at all! veranderd, betaald, ontdekt

Why? Because ge- is unstressed, and inseparable prefixes
take the stress position where ge- would go.

ver-: change/intensify
be-: make transitive / direct action at
ont-: undo / reverse / begin
her-: redo / again

steps.note:
ver- + anderen (other) = to change.
PP: veranderd (NO ge-!).
'De verandering' = the change.

steps.exampleEn: I want to improve my Dutch.
steps.note:
ver- + beteren (better) = to improve.
PP: verbeterd (NO ge-!).
'De verbetering' = the improvement.

be- + talen = to pay.
PP: betaald (NO ge-!).
You learned this early — now you know WHY
it's 'betaald' and not 'gebetaald'.

be- + schrijven = to describe.
PP: beschreven (NO ge-!).
'De beschrijving' = the description.

steps.example: Zij heeft een nieuw restaurant ontdekt.
steps.note:
ont- + dekken (cover) = to dis-cover!
Same logic as English: un-cover = discover.
PP: ontdekt (NO ge-!).

ont- + houden (hold) = to retain/remember.
PP: onthouden (NO ge-!).
'Onthoud dit!' = Remember this!

her- + halen (fetch) = to re-fetch/repeat.
PP: herhaald (NO ge-!).
'De herhaling' = the repetition/rerun.

steps.hint: ver-, be-, ont-, her- = no ge- in PP

steps.s: Kunt u dat {1}? Ik kan het niet {2}.
steps.hint: Repeat that? I can't remember it.

steps.title: Doe Maar Normaal
steps.desc: The unwritten rules of Dutch society. Gezelligheid, directness, doe normaal, birthday circles, and the tolerance paradox. This lesson explores what makes the Dutch... Dutch.

steps.note:
THE most Dutch word. No exact English translation.
Can mean: cozy, fun, sociable, pleasant, warm.
'Gezelligheid' = the state of being gezellig.
A café, a dinner, a person can all be 'gezellig'.

'Nuchter' = sober (not drunk) AND pragmatic.
'Nuchtere kijk' = pragmatic/sober view.
'Doe maar normaal' comes from this nuchterheid.

steps.en: just act normal / don't show off
steps.example: Doe maar normaal, dan doe je al gek genoeg.
steps.exampleEn: Just act normal, that's crazy enough.
steps.note:
THE Dutch saying. Don't be too flashy,
too loud, too proud, too different.
'Doe maar normaal, dan doe je al gek genoeg.'
= Just act normal, that's already crazy enough.

steps.title: 🇳🇱 Nederlandse Sociale Normen
steps.text:
1. Verjaardagen: Everyone sits in a CIRCLE.
You congratulate not just the birthday person,
but EVERYONE: 'Gefeliciteerd met je vader!'
= Congratulations with your father! (on his birthday)

2. Directheid: Dutch people say what they think.
'Dat is niet zo'n goed idee.' = That's not a great idea.
Not rude — just Dutch.

3. Afspraken: Always make an appointment.
Don't just 'drop by'. Even friends schedule visits.
'Zullen we een keer afspreken?' = Shall we meet sometime?

4. Gelijkheid: The Netherlands is very egalitarian.
The boss makes coffee. The intern gives opinions.
'Iedereen is gelijk.' = Everyone is equal.

5. Fiets: The bicycle is not transport — it's identity.
'Geen fiets? Geen Nederlander.'
= No bike? Not Dutch.

deepDive.title: Het Tolerantie-paradox
deepDive.text:
The Dutch are famous for tolerance:
early LGBTQ+ rights, legalized euthanasia,
coffee shops, the Red Light District.

But 'tolerance' in Dutch is more like:
'gedogen' = to allow/tolerate without approving.
It's pragmatism more than idealism.

And in recent decades, debates about immigration,
identity, and integration show that tolerance
has its limits and complexities.

The Netherlands is both very progressive
and more complicated than the stereotype.

opts: Cozy, fun, sociable, pleasant — depends on context
steps.ans: Cozy, fun, sociable, pleasant — depends on context

steps.s: ___ maar normaal, dan doe je al gek genoeg.

steps.q: At a Dutch birthday party, you congratulate:

steps.title: Unit 19 Review
steps.desc: Dutch traditions, environment, present and past participles as adjectives, compound nouns, inseparable prefixes, and cultural norms. Let's test it all.
goals: Inseparable prefixes: no ge- in PP

steps.s: De {1} bevolking groeit. Een {2} ei met brood.
steps.hint: Working population (present PP). Fried egg (past PP).
Don't show off, just be normal
